chunks.text: I'm not going to talk about this, seriously. Let me see Patrick. Oh, yeah. Oh. Mm-mm. You are really twisting something. Yeah. Well, me, you're too. I'm just kidding. Good morning. Good morning. All right. We are here this morning in live public session

for the Estimates for Vote Labour Market, in which we are hearing from the Minister of Education. Thank you, Minister, for being here and for all your officials as well. Immigration, did I not say that? Oh, did I? Oh, there you go. Sorry. I already said a couple of words around the wrong way earlier, so nothing special. Minister of Immigration, thank you very much for being here.

We have an hour with you this morning. We're open to introductory remarks, which we understand is a little extra to discuss, and so we'll hand it over to you now, and then we'll open for questions after that. Well, thank you very much for the opportunity to speak to you about Budget 2026 in the Immigration Portfolio. I'm joined today by Nick Blakely, Chief Executive of Embate.

As the committee have already been advised, my opening remarks will be slightly longer than usual, as there is a significant matter regarding the scrapping of Immigrations biometric capability upgrade project that I want to bring to your attention. This is a matter that I consider to be very serious, and that requires substantive explanation, both from myself and from Enby.

And as such, I'm going to focus my opening remarks on this, and I'll leave it to the committee to ask any other questions I would like about other decisions in Budget 2026, if you'd like to. I'm also tabling with the committee right now a review of the project that is being publicly released now. The BCU project.

by Metropathically Upratox School at BCU from now on began in 2018 to upgrade and future-proof immigration New Zealand's identity management systems, with a business case approved by NB in 2019. Estimated whole-of-life costs were $19.49 million, seemingly at this point with no ministerial oversight or sign-off of that $19 million. The project encountered challenges almost immediately. Critical decisions were made at pace without proper analysis due diligence. In the very early stages, there were already delays, unclear requirements,

rushed decisions, no proper governance and persistent disagreements. In 2020, the very critical decision was made by NB to significantly change the scope of the project to also include a workflow flow solution. So essentially taking it off the shelf solution and incorporating it together with a workflow solution, drastically changing the scope of the project. It significantly increased the complexity and the cost of the project, and it was done without proper analysis, interrogation, or importantly ministerial engagement oversight or approval.

These flawed foundations led to ongoing delivery issues, extensive customization, and a near constant stream of change requests. In total, more than 170 changes were raised, signalling deep structural issues with the project. Major change requests significantly altered scope, budget, timelines, and yet lacked analysis required to support these decisions.

Ministerial oversight was extremely limited, with most significant decisions being made internally within NB. In November 2021, Minister Farfoy and the Minister of Finance, Grant Robertson, noted the whole of life costs had increased to over $30 million, just noted.

Then in July 2023, Minister Little approved the increase to whole of life costs from the project from $30 million to $35 million, from the limited information available to me, it appears envy deliberately withheld information from those ministers and did not seek ministerial approval for whole-of-life cost increases when it was required throughout the project.

It is also not clear what questions previous ministers asked about the project on going delays and escalating costs or what information they received. Then just a month later, in August 2023, Minister Little declined to go to Cabinet seeking another whole-of-life increase to $40 million. In March 2024, officials recommended that I go to Cabinet seeking to increase the whole-of-life costs again without telling me that Minister Little had previously declined. Alongside this, importantly, they advised me that the project has undertaken

three independent quality assurance reviews, IQAs I'll call them. This last one being in September 2023. The latest IQA confirmed the project approach was sound and robust. The build was achievable and the risk management practice is effective. The project has implemented the IQA recommendations and continually looks for opportunities to improve.

I wasn't satisfied with the advice that I'd received and so I asked a number of questions, including seeking more information and asking for the IQA itself. It was at this point it became apparent to me that there were not only serious issues with the project, but what I had been advised about the IQA was complete fiction. Far from being sound and robust, achievable and effective, what the September 2023 IQA actually said was, given the project's poor delivery history, its ability, inability to meet agreed milestones,

we have doubts as to whether the project will in fact deliver at all. And we question its continuation. I was, as I'm sure that you can appreciate, furious, at being provided with advice that was diametrically opposed to the truth on such a critical project. However, I was also advised that ending the project would mean writing off $31 million of investment as well as exposing us to risks around the current system failing, and that the project was able to be delivered in just a few months. A range of...

significant interventions at this point were made, including an investigation by the chief executive of MB. Another IQA was commissioned, which returned an updated status possible. And I directed that an independent technology transformation expert be brought on the project to advise me directly. The chief executive of MB decided with those interventions in place alongside strength and governance to continue with the project given how advanced it was and that they expected to be able to go live within a couple of months,

a few months, sorry. I was also advised that MB would be able to keep within its originally within its whole of life set cost of 35 million. The project then made progress through the remainder of 2024 and 2025. We received regular updates on the progress from both MB and our independent advisor. And then in late 2025, as the project entered user acceptance testing, performance testing and system integration testing, problems compounded with more defects being created than were being resolved.

And ultimately, the decision was made by the MB acting chief executive in November 25. The project was irrefutable, could not go live and needed to be ended. So after seven years and $30 million, Budget 26 contains a repayable funding package that provides up to $31.2 million to write off the project, a similar amount to what I was advised in March 2024.

Those costs well over the next few years have to be met by future users of the immigration system. And it is totally unacceptable to me as a minister that after seven years, MB somehow spent tens of millions of dollars. Not only do we have nothing to show for it, but we are now in a position of having to maintain the existing aging infrastructure while a new solution is sought. I have more to say, including about the subsequent review and its damning findings that you now have. But I'm aware that the committee has already

given me an extended time, which I'm grateful for. And we can cover that in questions. First question from Phil Twyford. Minister, what is your view of MB's conduct through throughout this episode, and what do you intend to do about it? My view from what I have, the information that I have available, is that the report is very clear that ministers were not given full information, that in cases we were misled, that people were removed from the project when they asked questions about its viability.

And that the whole of life costs were not, they did not put to ministers to get proper approval, and information wasn't given to ministers. There's one more, the trifecta for me is people were moved sideways, ministers were misled or given poor information, but there was a third thing that will come to me in a moment.

I have spoken to the Public Service Commissioner on Friday. I've written to him, and I've asked him to undertake a review that he has agreed to, an integrity review, the terms of reference we provided to my office in the coming days. And I understand that MB will also be undertaking their own internal reviews as well, which Nick can speak to.

Do you have a view about how high this conduct went in the management hierarchy of MB? I don't, I'm not going to get into that, because I think that in the fullness of time the report will show us that. I remember the third thing, it's the trifecta.

Firstly, ministers were misled, secondly, people were moved from the project when they raised concerns, and thirdly, and this is quite serious, creative accounting practices have been undertaken to keep... costs of the project below cabinet's mandated limit. Those are extraordinarily serious findings of the report. They raise more questions than they answer, which is why I've asked Sir Brian to undertake a review. Can I make a few comments? Thank you. I'm extremely disappointed that it's taken seven years and $33 million and we have not delivered it. That's unacceptable. I found the review a

very difficult read. It finds serious failings and serious concerns about integrity, which the minister has raised. It does find that ultimately it was the correct decision to terminate the project, but that it could have happened much earlier. I take accountability for that is on behalf of the department over a long period of time and I'm committed to learning from this and implementing change. There are a number of actions underway. The first is Sir Brian's review into the integrity issues raised by this. He will look into that. On the back of that, I will undertake employment

investigations if that's appropriate on the basis of what that integrity review finds. I'm also taking a stocktake of all of our major technology projects to be assured that the governance and process failings that were evident in this review are not to be repeated in any of our other projects. I will also be strengthening our overall processes and getting independent assurance that those processes are now adequate. One of the other major immigration projects is the Future Services project and Sir Brian and the PSC will also be doing an assurance review

of that project. We're taking action to learn the lessons for this and to rectify things for the future. I've already apologized to the ministers and I would apologize to New Zealanders who expect this project. So minister you've you've alluded to the fact that because of the immigration system and particularly tax payers as well will have to pick up the tab 33 million dollars of waste of expenditure. Are there any other implications for users of the immigration system in terms of operational

benefits the fact that the planned benefits of the upgrade won't be whether the expected time frame. Yes and I can get officials to talk in more detail but at a very high level we've made sure that the existing technology is able to be maintained. When we officials are at the moment working on what is what a new solution is that they're going to be providing information to me in the near future and at that point we will take further decisions on what more we need to do to stabilise the existing technology but the advice that I've got is

at the moment the technology is stable and we likely will have to upgrade it somewhat depending on depending on the solution that we choose for the upgrade we may have to do a little bit more maintenance on it to make sure that it is stable because it is a very core part of our system. And do you have a plan to replace the upgrade project with a new project to deliver what was hoped for? Yeah I can talk to that. So yes the same issues that were the rationale for undertaking this project still exists in that our existing

biometric capability is old it is on systems that need upgrading so that still exists that's in place today it is still working it will need some additional investment over the coming years for it to keep going but there is still a case for having a more modern platform and that is the work that we are now undertaking that will be reporting to ministers in the next few months on.

You've given us a damning indictment of the conduct of even senior public servants in the MB. What questions are you asking about the behaviour and conduct of your officials outside of this project? If this behaviour has been demonstrated in relation to one IT project, what confidence do you have that these behaviours are not taking place in others?

Well, you may well remember Minister Andrew Little going down to the tiles and asking for an investigation in the dying days of the previous Labour Government on immigration because he wasn't getting the information that he needed and that led to the best work review, which again was very damning. And at that point, when I became the Minister, I said we've got a relationship to repair and my confidence at this point is low and we're going to need to repair that over the next few years.

And at that point was very clear, bring out everything that I need to see and we've done some great work over the last couple of years. But I am at a point now where that feels like it's been completely undone and undermined because of this that was not raised with me, even though we were unpicking it and finding out bits and pieces along the way it was never fully disclosed.

And also the fact that this report, you'll note, was dated two months ago and I received it on Friday. So those behaviours are seemingly still there and that's why I want a full investigation because while we've done a huge amount of great work in the policy space over the last couple of years, my confidence has been knocked again, which is very unfortunate.

So you've asked the Public Service Commission to do an investigation but what are you going to do about your department? Well, I can ask for the investigation and wait for the outcome of that but in the meantime, Nick has... has assured me that he's doing his part as well.

And I've already insisted that we are doing an overview of the current IT project upgrade, which is the R-Future Systems, which you'll know, and love is adept, moving things on to that to make sure that that business case is where it needs to be, that we're getting the outcomes that we expected and that that project is delivering as expected.

So my key concerns lie in the IT space, which is why I've asked for a full update of what's going on in those spaces as well. But we're talking about behaviors in relation to an IT project, but are you confident those same behaviors aren't being exhibited in the policy advice you're getting about compliance and enforcement, about the issuing of visas, about the humanitarian side of the department's work?

I mean, the advice that I've been getting from policy, I've been confident in. We've done a huge amount of good work over the last few years, and you see that from the turnaround of migrant exploitation, of numbers, of skills. And I'm very happy with that work.

However, there is certainly a side in NB. And remembering that this IT department sits outside of immigration itself in the wider NB environment. And this is something that none of my DCEs had a direct line of sight over. And that's part of the problem. But the advice I've been getting on policy relating to our work program over the last two years, I've been very satisfied with.

OK, thank you. Because I take integrity very seriously, and that's why I welcome the public service commissioners review. I see expectations with my team. This is not where we should be as a department. And so I will be doing everything I can to give a fair statement.

A supplementary question. Thanks, here. I've got a couple of clarifying questions coming out of what? I'm pretty upright. just situations being read. Am I correct in understanding that the $31.2 million, I think I heard that's getting written off, is approximately where the cost was at when you became the minister?

The advice I received on the 28th of March was that that would be the write-off cost. But to be honest with you, I think there is lots of questions to be asked around the actual costs of this project because of the report and its claims around creative accounting. So I think there'll be a lot to be dug out.

Obviously, ministers, you have to go on the advice that they're given. The 28th of March sent a $31 million write-off cost, which is significant, especially given where it was so close to the end of the project. But yes, those two figures are very similar. There was a number of things that Enby were doing in the background to recover.

They were getting clawing back funds from the vendor and assuring me they could work within the whole of life costs that had already been set. On page 18, I've just quickly read this report. It says that with some staff characterizing financial management practices as creative accounting, largely driven by efforts to avoid exceeding the cabinet-level funding threshold.

That's a pretty outrageous statement to make in a report. As the minister, how does that make you feel about the department's performance? Well, this is almost as bad as it gets, avoiding cabinet scrutiny. There is more to this, though, and uncovering all of the documents that previous ministers signed out when it came to whole of life costs, it was minimal.

There was only two signouts by ministers across the life of the project when it went from $19 million to $35 million eventually. There was only two signouts. And it's supposed to be. to be signed out every time that goes, the whole of life cost goes up as far as I've been advised and that seemingly did not happen and even when it did happen the advice was very minimal and I'll give you an example of one of those where Minister Farf always signed out a piece of paper that that's how big it is, it's a pamphlet, it doesn't provide any of the advice and I've not seen

any other advice that's been able to be provided to me that was received by either him or the Minister of Finance so the advice was very scant it did not lay out the history of the project or the increase in costs and actually by the time it seems from what I can see the only I'm only going on the information I've got by the time they were asked to to sign out an approval to go over 20 million dollars a whole of life cost it had already gone to 30 million so there's there is a whole lot of things that needs to be looked into because I don't think that ministers

were getting the information and the advice that they should have got when making these decisions I will certainly be asking more questions to the department later but a question for you how do you think Merlin, the physiotherapist and woman who bring in physios from overseas or Patrick who's bringing in IRC workers for forestry or Neil and Harah who's bringing in Filipinos as managers are going to feel when they understand that they're going to be paying not only for the past project on those visa costs but the next one and as much as Nick I respect the fact that you've

acknowledged that you're not happy with this has it actually been worked out as a what it's going to cost per person what this is actually costing every one of those visa holders in the future to pay for this mistake look no it hasn't we haven't got to that stage yet in fact we haven't actually done the write-off as yet and I think there will be more to come out of this actual costs and of course we don't know what the future an IT solution will look like at this point or what the costs will be of maintaining the current biometric capability system, but it certainly is a

very difficult pill to swallow that those costs will be borne by future users of the system, as I said in my opening speech. Supplementary, Ricardo. Yeah, just good a few questions. Thank you. On page 17, the report notes that the cybersecurity risks were significant. Were you at any point being made aware that there were high cybersecurity risks attached to this project?

Well, the whole point of the project is to avoid those. And one of the things that we were advised in early 2024 was the fact that if we didn't complete the project, then those risks would be born. And that's quite a serious thing, not only the right of the $31 million, but also the fact that we had a legacy system that at the time we were told was at serious risk and those and those would be the implications.

But I mean, what does it say that in this project with NEC, like there were a lot of cybersecurity risks that were significant according to the report and you were kept out of the loop when it's something that could massively impact people's rights to privacy or information?

Does it does it not raise significant concerns that there were cybersecurity risks attached to this project and you were not made aware of those? There's lots of concerns. I'll probably get into respect to this because it's quite technical if that's OK. Yeah, just to clarify, this was of the potential solution. So it was never put into practice. So the concerns were about this, the work that was being done on the project so that they were never exposed into individuals and our existing system is still in place.

And can I just ask what communications did NB or yourself minister have with any migration five partners in relationship to, for example, this project or the issues that have arisen since the report and what feedback have you received? if, you know, I have other migration five countries had similar issues with this company or with trying to update the systems, which would be able to align any sort of communications you've had with the partners that would have been part of the secure real time platform information sharing network.

So I'm not sure about M5 communication over the period of us, we can get back to that. On the, I may just say something about the vendor and the nature of this project, there were there were two main parts to the project. There was a core functionality around image matching, essentially, so you are an intensity matching.

And then there's how you then, the workflow was the minister mentioned at the start about how you integrate that into these systems and the core functionality, you know, any C would be regarded as global best practice. They're actually that functionalities of the core of our existing system. It's also, I believe, in DIAs, all of government solution, social recognition as a service.

So what was unique about this was building, asking the vendor to build that referral that was, I understand, unique at the time. And that was, as the minister said, the core mistake early on was to go down that route. So just to distinguish between the core technology that NEC holds versus this particular workflow that was being developed.

So a very succinct supplementary, Vanessa, thank you, minister. Yeah, have you any concerns in this project about fraud? Is there any potential for that given that one of the project costs, as said, create accounting. So inconsistent financial reporting, make it a definitive view of actual expenditure and movements of 4.44 million transferring between.

Are you confident that there has been no fraud in this? Look, I can't speak to Ford, the PSC will do their investigation, but from the information that I've been provided, what I can tell so far, and I still don't have a full picture, and from what I've been told, things were done that required cabinet oversight in order to keep it away from Cabinet.

So splitting the project into two parts again and keeping it like that, a required cabinet oversight. But for some reason, that decision was taken by the government's body overseeing the project and kept away from ministers. So all I can go by is the report that I received literally a couple of days ago before you have now seen it, but this is part of the investigation that the PSC will need to undertake.

OK, quickly grant. Thank you. Thank you. It's a very confusing situation. But then I'm just, what surprises me is that at no point within the ministry itself, envy itself, that there was not a big red flags coming up to the senior management level, particularly this project, it's seven years, it seems to be an ardent long time to work through a project of this sort of nature.

Any comment on that? I mean, I think this is where the review finds the serious failings in the oversight and governance, that it didn't have the sufficient oversight. In fact, decisions went around governance. There wasn't sufficient reporting to ministers. That wasn't even always correct when it did hurt to ministers.

So it's those systems and processes that were not in place, were not adhered to. That is the review find that behind the failings. Thank you. I have a final supplement. Thank you. Since this finding, are there any other technology project systems and processes that are being red flagged?

So we have a number of technology programs. There is, I do recall, one of them not in the immigration space is subject to examination at the moment about that one's on track. But that is why I want to leave an overall stock take of all of the projects. Do they have the appropriate governance oversight, reporting some ministers, if there are any concerns, we flag those up.

So for stock take, how far back is it going to look at? That's the current project, all of the current in-train technology projects. Okay, just before we move on, Ricardo. Yeah, the reporters are pretty damning on any see the company. And just from a quick search, it seems like it's used by several other countries.

Do you have any concerns based on the experience that in behind with this company that, you know, this being used by other countries, which we have information sharing regimes with that risks attached with us sharing information with countries that have an alignment with a company that has had a multitude of issues with its ability to, you know, deploy this kind of technology issues with cybersecurity.

And do you not have any concerns about the fact that other countries that we share information with are using NEC as well? I'll start with high level and I'll get Nick to expand. But if we had stuck with the original scope of buying the off the shelf project product, it's very likely that none of this would have happened.

The core critical problem was in 2020 when MB took the decision to turn what was an off the shelf perfectly usable IT system and merge it with a workflow system that had not been done before. So the advice that I've received is that it is used by us, it's used by other countries and we'll have to continue to use it. The critical failure was that merging that in hindsight should never have happened. And that's what caused all of the potential problems, which is why we we couldn't integrate it at the end and why it eventually

wasn't able to be integrated with the system. Had it been integrated into the system, then you would have seen some of those issues. Yeah, it goes back to the best two parts. Is the core technology, the ability to do the facial matching, that's what's used around the world. It was this workflow path, and that's where the cyber security issues come from, because they come from interface with the rest of the world.

Would you mind just expanding on exactly why the relationship between immigration in New Zealand and NEC was strained? The bottom page forward just has a line on that, but that seems quite serious, that not only was that a lot of this was kept from the ministers, but that even throughout the relationship between Ayn said and NEC was not good.

Do you mind just broadening what issues immigration in New Zealand encountered in dealing with the company? Yeah, I think this goes back to the setting what the project was not set up well. And so then what needed to happen was a number of changes through the process. It wasn't clear on the requirements at the start.

Again, there were a few thoughts about that. And all of those things meant for a strange relationship, because you're constantly changing what's required and renegotiating. It's those sorts of things all along the way, trying to resolve those defects, perhaps differences of view about how best to resolve those.

I'm not close to all the ins and outs, but it's those types of things made for a very challenging relationship. As you saw, as I mentioned when I started this, over 117 change requests does not make for a great relationship or a stable project. But I think that the key thing that the report finds was around right at the very beginning, the business case, lacked the detailed analysis and required to make support decision making.

It lacked due diligence, market testing, and despite the scale of complexity of the project. So you can see that right from the business case, not being set up well, and then that massive change of scope in 2020, essentially, of the Shelf Act and merged it with the workflow system was a critical failure.

Before we move on, can I just ask why was the Minister not aware of the report for two and a half months? That's the one thing that's sticking out that hasn't been answered for me. So that's on us. We received the report, and my focus actually at the time was fully on the fuel response. The team at the time decided that it would be better to have a response to the report at the same time reporting to Ministers. That was the wrong call, but that's why that was made, and it took too long to get through.

Can I just note, though, in that preceding two months, there was no response provided. Yeah, we've noted that. Right. Next primary, new topic. Thank you. Two years ago, Minister, you substantially increased immigration fees. Dumbled, doubled many of them, which was a big shock to many users of the immigration system, but it was done on the basis that the system should be financially sustainable and largely funded by its users. Less than two years later, the budget provides $31.2 million of ground support and access to an $84 million contingency.

What are the principal reasons that the system is currently not financially self-sustaining? Were visa revenues low as an expected expenditure higher than expected? The advice that I've received, and I'll get officials to come and speak to this as well, the main contributing factor is the volume of the AEW visa was far, far lower than they predicted over that time. They lower expected volumes. They also failed to take into

account the products that we don't charge for because of bilateral relationships or you'll know you'll be aware of them as to why there's many of them and potentially a bit of an over-optimism of what the visa volumes would be but I have been assured and I've been driving this myself to ensure that we are improving our productivity and I've been advised that their internal budgets have had good cost controls. It's not usual for a minister to get involved in operations but when I discovered that, for example, the Christchurch branch had very low productivity

in terms of their job check, for example, they're processing on average of one a day or something similar whereas Auckland were doing four a day, immediately ensured that that was dealt with and so the systems and processes in immigration now are much different making sure we're improving productivity. So while we've had declining numbers of visas over this difficult fiscal period the team have been making sure that we are improving productivity and output so that we are competitive. The issues lie with reduced numbers of visas and also which we probably

should have expected before we made the change. We had a massive surge, you may remember, in visitor visa applications so a whole lot of visa applications massive surge took us a while to get through but then obviously didn't see the benefits of those increases so the team have gone away and ensured that they're on top of those now for the next review. Would you agree that it amounts to a pretty significant failure of forecasting and if so what are you doing about improving the department's financial forecasting? Well yes it is a failure and I think they will admit that

themselves and we've made sure that in the upcoming fee and levy review that all of the things that were not calculated correctly will be dealt with. I would say though that it is like Treasury encounter occasionally it is very difficult to predict the future in terms of visa volumes. They do their very best. I don't think we definitely didn't get it right. Could we have done better? Absolutely, but there is an element of crystal ballgazing when it comes to visa volumes but I know that the team have gone away and worked

on what went wrong last time to make sure that that's moved out. So who do you expect to ultimately bear the cost of restoring the immigration visa memorandum account? Employers, migrants or taxpayers? Well it'll be the future users of the immigration system because we are a self funding immigration system and those costs will be borne by future users of the immigration system. So in saying that are you signalling hefty future increases in visa fees and levies to be paid by migrants and employers over the next year or two? Not necessarily and this is outside of my

expertise and I can get the team come up and talk about it but we are of course forecasting higher visa volumes and you may remember in 2021 when a similar write-off or similar loan was done in terms of a write-off. It only accounted for part of the deficit of the two accounts.

The remaining balance of those accounts was borne by future visa holders and an increased number of people coming through the system and that's what you will see here as visa numbers pick up and we can already see that they're doing that that will also help. Do you have a plan to restore the immigration system to a fully sustainable cost recovery basis? Yes we do and that will be borne out by the future fee and levy review of course it's 100% the fee and levy review that's coming up and the work that we're doing to improve our productivity

and that's what part of the big part of our future services moving everything on to a depth to get those that productivity up and we saw that when we put the PRV visa on to ADEPT. We had huge increases in productivity so that will be part of it but also making sure that we're staying on top of productivity of our different offices and the team are doing a really good job of that.

Another supplementary, oh no, supplementary, Ricardo Menendez. Yeah, thank you. You mentioned that there's a few levers that could be used to our guests boost a dollar figure including higher intake of migrants or higher fees. How are you hoping to address leveraging of a higher intake of migrants to make up for this and where do you hope this would come from if part of the forecasting was wrong and one of the solutions is to just have more migrants. Have you done any work to consider where that revenue would be coming from? Well, can I bring my officials up?

It's less about deciding to have more migrants and more about what will the likely volumes be in going forward. So making the system add up is a combination of the price, the visa fee, the volume coming in and then the costs of the system and what the minister said is we need to get better at our forecasting on the volumes and as the economy recovers, you would expect volumes to be increasing and we need to not overestimate that which is what happened last time. We took too much account of the recent surge at the time of the AWV visas and overestimated what was coming.

So we need to be careful and as the minister said, we've been strengthening some of the how we do our forecasting. So that's the volumes and then the cost control is how do we so our appropriation is not materially increasing over the coming years. We've been keeping that constant as we look for these positive improvements. So we do as much cost control as we can. We do our best forecasting on the dams, then the last part dams.

for what happens on price and over what timeframe to be able to get back to a sustainable level. Just got a different point of question. Oh, okay, I have, yeah, okay. Okay, just on page 180 of the vote labor on the, facing some of your goals, you have a new measure now on that's replacing the number of people liable for deportation who were deported or departed voluntarily after compliance activity.

You've increased that number to 2,000 added status regularization. Yes. Would you, would you mind outlining whether you, what the breakdown of those are and whether that extra 500 would come from status regularization or, you know, what's it just mostly a higher number of deportations that you're aiming for?

I will talk at a high level and I've got Steve here who can talk to the detail. In the past, the 1500 figure only included deportations either forced deportations, people self-deporting or voluntary removal, there's three different categories. And part of our work is not only to deport people but it's to regularize them as well, which means, hey, you know, have you put in a section 61?

Have you tried this route to try and get them back? Especially if they've been here a really long time they've stayed a long time to try and get them back on a pathway of being here legally. And that is a big part of our work and it's never been reflected in those numbers. And so increasing that to 2,000 to take account of the fact that that is quite a big part of our work and also to celebrate the fact that, yes, we are getting people off being here illegally and onto valid visas is important.

However, and I'll get Steve to talk to the detail, but I do also want to state that it is my intention and I've made it clear that when it is time to leave, it is time to leave. And we have increased our deportations year on year on year, people who, and we always start with our top priority and our priorities haven't changed.

Our top priority is those people who have undertaken illegal activity. so our criminals, then going down to people with character issues and then it goes down a scale and predominantly the people that we deport, the people who are undertaken criminal activity, Steve can talk to more of that and that is really important to ensure the integrity of the immigration system and I stand by that and I'm really proud of the fact that actually we have been able to deport more people over the last few years given that they are still working really very

much at the top of that priority scale. I'll get Steve to talk more about your question about the breakdowns. Yes, thank you. We've tested that for a couple of years with sort of some business measures so for the, I don't have the exact figures but I can give you a pretty good approximation, so last financial year the measure was, we achieved about 2,200 what I would call resolutions, I think about 1,200 of those would have been deportations and the other 1,200 were regularisations and I, as the minister said, it's a legitimate part of compliance work to get people to comply

and complying can include getting back on track and regaining a lawful status so they, another way of putting it I guess is that those people who were unlawful are now lawful so they have complied. Just, the minister mentioned that, you know, immigration in New Zealand may offer people a pathway to regularise themselves, how much are you practically offering those pathways or, you know, if they're petitioning no data, do you actually practically ever offer those routes to people? So the compliance officer's practice is to

case manage individuals and part of that is quite an extensive understanding of the personal circumstances of the person, so when a compliance officer has done it, for example, a humanitarian interview or they've had an opportunity to engage. with the client, they may invite that person to apply for Section 61.

You know often that's happening because my experience with constituents is that that has not been the case, that most of the time people are just being basically asked to arrange a way to go back home, not to actually have the compliance officer offer sort of like a Section 61 route or to guide them that they can go to the Minister.

How often are you actually offering those regularization pathways because I'm assuming the numbers that you have lying around the breakdown will come from people going to lawyers, to MPs, to other parts of the sort of immigration ecosystem but not so much immigration in New Zealand actually offering those regularization pathways. That's quite a lot of detail but I'll go back to the statistic of half of the people that we engage with get back onto a compliant pathway regularization and that is as a result of the case

management of the compliance officers. Okay, cool. We have a supplementary from Pamje and we'll move on to this. So the investment targeting overstay is here and the estimated number that is known to us is around 21,000. So I want to know how are you planning to measure success. So are we, we should be looking forward to seeing the number coming down in how many years, what's going to look like. Can I make an opening statement before I hand over to Steve? We have a historic problem. When the borders reopened the best review lays this out.

We had, I mean I said at the time in opposition our borders were compromised. We weren't properly verifying documents or applying the proper risk management practices and at that time we had lots, thousands of people into the country, many of which who are still here, who are coming up to the end of their maximum continuous state and may already have become illegal or who will shortly be.

So we have quite a big historic issue that is coming upon us. I'm aware of it, Steve's aware of it, but I just wanted to make the point before we go into that detail, that not only are we dealing with people who may have been here for a very long time, but we're also dealing with people who came in during a period of time after COVID and after the borders reopened, that I am expecting that we will have to intervene.

So to answer your question around the immigration unlawful list, the list itself is used for a number of purposes, but it's not, we don't use it to determine who we deport. It's used to understand statistical analysis and to help policy development. It is based on a number of data sets and we're constantly working to refine those data sets and to answer your question, do we want to come down?

Of course we do, yeah. It's used, it's an estimate, but as a measure of success, I would expect it to reduce over time. Okay, thank you. New primary fill, Twyford. I'm keen to know the latest numbers on the amount invested. How much of that has been invested so far has gone into vehicles like managed funds, private credit listed entities, bonds and equivalent structures?

You can start up, I'll just need to write. Yeah, as at 31 March, there's 1.48 billion actually committed. Yep, a further 2.2 in the pipeline, but of that 1.48 billion that is committed, the split between growth and balance is 982 million for growth and 492 for balanced.

within growth around overall managed funds is $725 million and then there are various sub-categories of that. How much directly invested in New Zealand businesses? $20 million of the $982 million in direct investments. Okay. Can I make a point here? Because I know where you're going Phil. All of this money is flowing down into New Zealand businesses while a large chunk of it is going into private credit. We keep a very, very close eye. We are re-accrediting businesses funds every three months to make sure it's being deployed. We're

watching where it's being deployed and there are many companies who do not want to give away equity. In fact, I was down in Dunedin with one United Machinists recently where they had investment from private credit. Family business do not want to give away equity and need a line of credit that is flexible that allows them not only to purchase the technology that they want, but also to send their people offshore to do professional learning and development, to do marketing, which they would never get from the bank. So while you're asking a question

about flowing directly into businesses, all of these funds are flowing into businesses. So off the $725 million I think that you said have gone into a range of different vehicles. Of that money, how much has that has actually been invested through credit or equity and it's not sitting in those vehicles? Well, as I've just said earlier to you, we do a check every three months to make sure that money is being deployed.

So we've got a really careful balance because if we say to companies you need to deploy it immediately, they'll make poor decisions. So there is always a flow of money that's sitting waiting to be deployed, but we go and check all of these funds every three months to ensure that they are deploying and when they are not, they're out. So I think it's two. How much has it gone out?

You're checking every few months, you must know how much has gone out and how much is still wet. I've got an update from Inves New Zealand. Remember, I'm not responsible for Inves New Zealand, but they update me. So they've just gone out and done their re-accreditation and they're about to come back to me next month to let me know what the state of play is at the moment. But the money is flowing constantly in and out, but I'm going to get Stacey to come up because Stacey is our resident expert and sits across the top of this. She'll be able to explain more about it. It's not as simple

as being able to go this much is sitting here and this much is out because it's a constant flow, but I'll let you explain it. Yeah, I don't have the exact figures of how much has been deployed and what is sitting in cash, but we'll just reiterate what the minister has said. You don't need to reiterate it, but my question is if you're checking every few months about how much money has gone out either in form of credit or equity to New Zealand businesses, you must know at any one point in time. Well, we'll be able to tell you next week.

Why don't you know now? Isn't that important information? Yeah, I just don't have the figures to very quick to want to generate headlines about four billion dollars has been committed and pipeline funds, but you can't tell the committee. You can't tell Parliament how much of it is actually we can tell you the old fill will tell you the old figure and we'll be able to tell you the new figure next week. But I am confident because I made the checks be every three months rather than what did we start out every six every three months. And people are being having their accreditation

revoked when they are not deploying and we're doing it quickly. So they know, but it is always that balance to make sure we're not on top of them too much and saying, hey, you've got to deploy immediately because they will make bad decisions and bring the entire thing into disrepute. But from the information that we've been given Every time I get updated, every three months, money is being deployed.

And if it is not, people are being budgeted out. Next week, we'll be able to provide this committee with the up-to-date figures of deployment. OK, but at the moment, what you've told us is that of $1.48 billion committed, $20 million has been directly invested in New Zealand businesses.

And you can't currently tell us how much has been deployed of the $725 million that has gone to a range of different financial vehicles. We'll get that information to you. There will be the vast majority, but yes, we can get that. Most of it will have gone out. All right, I'll look forward to seeing that. But like I say, we keep on top of it, and all of that money is flowing through into New Zealand businesses.

Would you agree that if money is sitting around for a year in a vehicle, which has been, there have been news reports that that has actually happened, that is a big chunk of the three-year period that these funds are committed? Well, it's important here at this point to make the point that the previous settings, you could have your money sitting in cash for 75% of the time, but it only had to be fully invested for one year of those four years.

We changed that. We had to discuss your scheme. I know, but I'm just making the point that we wanted money to be deployed as soon as possible. It was not acceptable to me that under the previous settings, that you only had to have your money fully deployed for one year. That was not acceptable. So we've moved it right back to the start and said the clock starts ticking straight away, but that has been part of the success of this, not only that, but some of the other system changes as well.

It is phenomenally successful. I mean, there's over $4 billion of investment that has flowed into the country because of this. A lot of it's still waiting to be deployed because we're going through the application process. But the one point. All right. All right. That's been deployed already as significant.

Well, we look forward to seeing the number that's actually... On that point you made about money that's sitting around for a year, I'll get you to speak to that more broadly because wherever we see that happening, we take immediate action and you'll remember dims. We closed down dims immediately.

We gave the bad actors plenty of warning and when they did not comply, I made a decision and they were told the next day that it was over. We're taking a very hard-line approach to anybody who is trying to get around the system and sit on cash and that's evident by the fact that we're checking every three months and if money is not being deployed, we are taking people's accreditation away to ensure the integrity of the system but it is a very careful balance to make sure we're not pushing them to deploy too quickly, giving them a reasonable time.

How long is it acceptable for money to sit around and not be deployed in your view? So what invest NZ does is ask for managed funds around what is their pipeline. So the money coming in, have they got a plan to match that pipeline? If they don't then discussions they had between those managed funds and the options are to suspend or revoke them from the list.

So they would expect to see firstly that pipeline and then a change in deployment every three months. So that's discussed. We have a monthly meeting with Minister Stanford and invest NZ so it's actively being monitored and the more information we get then we can take that to provide advice in terms of should the settings change to ensure that money is going and meeting the intent of this visa.

We have a review. It was supposed to be an 18 month review and I pulled it forward to a 12 month review coming up to make sure that we stay on top of all of these issues, the integrity of the system and making sure that money is being deployed. is my top priority which is why I meet with invest New Zealand officials every month to understand or as often as I can to understand money to be deployed and take very quick decisions and at the time for the people who were involved in DIMMS, pretty unpopular decision for them but maintaining the integrity of the system and making sure money is being

deployed is my top priority. We have a very quick supplementary from Grant and Karl who have got to move on to our final primary. Thank you. When you set up the activist plus scheme or readjusted it, you would have had expectations of how you know what levels of success would look like. How do they compare with we have actually gone?

It has exceeded what we expected. I think I remember saying we wanted about 200. We expected to see 200 applications in the first year and I think we blew past 600. That is good but it also creates challenges for us as well. Recently we made some announcements around adding philanthropy and with this 12-month review we will be looking at what else we need to do to ensure that there are enough acceptable investments in growth to make sure that there are plenty of opportunities for this $4 billion that is coming down at us.

Of course not all that $4 billion is going into growth but we're keeping a very close eye on it. We keep a very close eye on all of those managed funds and where they are deploying to. It has been really good to see that we have had a number of infrastructure funds pop up that are now investing in things like aged care and student accommodation which is new and some of the private credit is now going into infrastructure as well. As it matures, that is why I am a bit nervous to make too many changes and muck around with it when it

is still maturing and we are starting to see different things come online. We saw a very early start people were very nervous about direct investments in private equity but that has people are starting to take more risks. So I am a little bit nervous about touching it too much, just while it is still maturing, but we're seeing some really incredible outcomes and businesses that are being able to expand and grow and have new export markets and do marketing and all sorts of things with funds that they wouldn't have had otherwise.

And private credit, especially in New Zealand, was very immature and it is now being able to mature. And we are keeping a very close eye on exactly where it's all going to make sure it's doing what it should be. Just the private credit area is an area that has been, that growth capital has been underdone part of the capital market.

So actually, this is quite a positive sign to see that that's growing. There's actually 96 million in venture capital. That's an area that's a key part of the early stage as well, so there's some very promising signs. Okay, final step from Carl and then final question. So minister, just to be clear, where people are not following the rules, you are revoking those visas. So there is that accountability.

This goes back to compliance. I can get Steve back. In this budget, you will see there was funds set aside for more compliance. Predominantly, that compliance is around for practices by employers. We have a huge list that we struggle to get to with the current teams.

And this introduces three new teams, which will allow us to do a lot more of the investigations and the compliance and issuing of infringement notices to ensure that we have good behavior of our employers, but also obviously in doing that work, people who are working illegally because they've been allowed to by the business who's employing them, well, slash doubt.

But ultimately this funding is to ensure that the integrity of the system is maintained via the employer. Come on. Final question from Ricardo. What preparations, if any, is your office making to deal with the increased number? of ministerial intervention requests that will will likely come as a result of the enhanced risk management amendment bill due to people not being able to go to the IPT.

Well, some of the many of those people still will be able to remember we talked about the fact that a lot of those people will be given one day visas to be able to access the IPT. So a large portion of those will actually still end up in the IPT, especially those people who are very long, long-term overstages, a bank chunk of those. I can get the officials to talk more to this, but whenever we see volumes increase in ministerials, we increase the resource available through our delegated decision makers. So there's a range of things that can happen.

So you're not expecting to change any of your processes in your office on how you handle ministerial intervention requests as a result of the bill? I don't deal with ministerial intervention requests. Well, your associates work, yeah. Okay, we'll just then move on to who we have next, and we've actually come to the end of our time. Wonderful. Thank you very much, minister and to the officials. I appreciate your participation. We have the associate minister next. I'm assuming most of the officials will be here, but I'll let you reset as you
go.

Good morning, welcome, Casey, the Associate Minister for Immigration. We have you here for 40 minutes, and to the officials who have already been with us. Thank you for staying on. We have it with 40 minutes, my calculation, about two minutes for introductory remarks.

We'll leave that to you and then we'll go to questions after that. Thank you. Good morning, Chair and Minister of the Committee. As Associate Minister of Immigration, I'm responsible for our refugee program and for the work combating, trafficking and persons. I'm just going to make some brief comments. The nature of forced displacement and migration continues to evolve, with record numbers of people worldwide seeking safety and better opportunities.

As global responses change, New Zealand is now the third largest resettlement country after Canada and Australia have been menstruating sustained leadership in an increasingly uncertain international environment. I'd like to stress that that isn't the third largest per capita, with the global system under strain. New Zealand continues to meet its resettlement commitments, and we take the third largest number of UNHCR refugees.

Our approach remains rooted in our values of fairness, dignity and support for those in need. If we review our strategies and programs from the refugee quota program, family support and our work with asylum seekers and victims of trafficking, we reaffirm the dedication to providing pathways for protection and integration. New Zealand maintains a strong and long-standing relationship with the UNHCR, working together to support and protect the rights and wellbeing of refugees and displaced populations globally.

I'm pleased that INZ has met the annual target of proving 600 people under the Refugee Family Support Programme and the Refugee Quota Programme, reaching the on-track to meeting the 1500. I'm also pleased to inform you of progress made of those individuals who have been waiting in the Tier 2 registration queue.

As at May 2025, there was 3,737 people in the Tier 2 registration queue. As at the same date this year, the number has dropped to 2,180. Finally, as you know in December 2025, the government released the new All of Government Transnational Serious and Organised Crime Strategy and Action Plan. This aims to create a more cohesive response to transnational Serious Organised Crime. One of the actions in the strategy pertains to trafficking in persons with INZ leading the development of a standalone Trafficking in Persons National

Action Plan. This plan will provide a prioritised and coordinated multi-agency approach to enhancing New Zealand's response to trafficking. Thank you. Thank you very much. We have a first question from Phil Twyford. Good morning Minister. I wanted to ask you about the Refugee Family Support category. The Immigration New Zealand's Refugee Survey makes it very clear that family connections are among the strongest determinants of successful refugee settlement points made very clearly in the report. In light of that, it's good

to hear that progress has been made on working through the pool of registrations for the Refugee Family Support category. Of the reduction from, you noted, from 3,725 down to 2,180 in the pool, how many of those were registrations that were cleared because people had aged out or moved to Australia? How much of that reduction is due to people who have been processed, their registrations have been processed and have led to sponsorships? Yeah, and I will confirm that the sponsor is no longer eligible to be a sponsor. There's been a range

of things that have impacted on that total number. I would refer to the fact that we've had 80... registrations which is 316 people have resulted in an invitation to apply to apply 36 registrations which is 185 people were declined and I might return to officials around their numbers of sitting outside of that. So yes tier 2s do have a reasonably high decline rate and when we assess that sponsor for eligibility it's around half of registrations and a quarter of applications when they get through to RTA. There are lots of reasons why as you say some

of them why they don't meet instructions any longer sometimes they no longer live in New Zealand sometimes they don't meet the criteria to have lived here in the last three years and sometimes their dependence the people they want to sponsor have aged out. So just want to be clear. Just a quick clarification how many were aged out proportion-wise? So you didn't mention that sorry?

I'm not sure exactly but I think it's around 20% but I'd have to check. Yeah so I think it's 1,545 is the reduction if my math is right. How many of those of those of that 1,545 are progressing through the system when the distribution is being processed and how many lead to actual sponsorships? So the actual 60 there was 15 applications which represents 60 people were approved and there's 88 registrations representing 316 people of results and invitations to apply. Okay right thank you. Based on that experience over the last year do you have

a better sense than you did a year ago of the waiting time the average waiting time for people in the pool of registrations? I think the challenge is even those that have been invited to apply we still estimate about three years to work through those invitations to apply so we still believe there's a number of years to work through that's why we're still not reopening the category. So three years to process from when they're invited to apply.

Then these people have been waiting for how many years on average? The queue isn't, the people in the queue were pulling at the moment were waiting for nine years. For nine years. When he said in teen when it was last open. Right. But there's two things that that will determine the timing. So one is how many tier ones come in because that's perpetually open and we we have to prioritize those because they're people with no immediate family in New Zealand.

And the second one is the conversion rate. So we we put out the first large tranche of ITAs invitations to apply for tier two in September last year and they have 12 months. So we won't know until September how many of those are lapsing and how many are converting into residence applications. So that's there's quite a lot of uncertainty in our forecast.

So so given your refugee report really underlined how critical family connections are for successful settlement. Have you asked for advice on what it would take to clear the backlog within a reasonable timeframe and reopen tier two for registrations?

It is actually beyond that. I've actually it is part of the Immigration New Zealand's policy work to actually review the policy positions on this. So it is in this year's policy work program for Immigration New Zealand to actually look at this whole system because as you say this category isn't I don't think it's fit for purpose. Yeah. And I think it's one something we've been grappling with for a long time and given exactly that point that we are trying to optimise the settlement and settle as well as possible. I think this this program and those waiting

and because we've had people waiting so long. Yeah. You don't want to open it to new people until they're waiting. has been worked through. So this is why Immigration New Zealand, we have committed that to the policy work to be undertaken this year. And Minister, so for that policy work, are you considering both options that might accelerate and expand the opportunities for settlement under tier two, as well as options that might truncate it and reduce options for settlement?

Well, I think the first principle basis is that exactly as you said, we want people to have the best outcomes for settlement in New Zealand and we know that family is an important part of that. I don't want to predeterminate or direct where this policy work may take us, but as I've said previously in this committee, I think it's really unfair to have this system where people are waiting so many years on the hope. And given that we've, you know, this is the first time we've had a big tranche. So I think that's the whole process around what is the best way to support

family unification within the program that we've got. I accept that you agree it's important, but you could make a failing or problematic policy less problematic in a policy sense by truncating and reducing the opportunities. Or you could resource it up and expand it and make those opportunities more accessible or create more opportunities. Both are logically possible, right? Yeah, exactly. Both are logically possible.

Well, I mean, those both options, one has significant budget implications. So I think they are presentable as one of those options, but it is again, you know, what is the commitment that we can make as a government to expanding this program and what is the sacrifices you make to achieve that? So I'm not preempting anything around what is the position, but as a policy development process, of course, we've costings attached.

both programs. So I'm not saying one or the other, I'm just saying that we need to work through the policy settings in this space. Are you finally on this? Are you expecting to have policies that might make its way to cabinet before the end of the year? Not within this term, no. Thank you. Ricardo Menendez-Bach.

Yeah, thank you. Minister, when you mentioned that these would have significant budget implications, have you actually explored these budget implications? So obviously, we're not the wiser of how much money would need to be put into it. It would be impossible to explore budget obligations when we don't have policy settings to explore. It would be, we have to have policy settings around waters that we're trying to achieve, and then that is costed, but we're not at that stage yet.

Councillor Humfryes. Thank you. The community refugee sponsorship scheme. Has cabinet made a decision on the long-term future of the forwards program? No, there's no decisions as yet. We are progressing, as I've said last time, to get to that point.

I'm looking forward to taking a paper to cabinet to get that through, but no decisions have been made and no cabinet paper has been put forward as yet. So the contract for Host International, who have oversight of the program, runs out I think in September. Has MV assessed the risk and the potential cost of lost expertise within the program? There's 28 participating community groups who have now been left hanging for a year with no certainty of funding or support.

Are you concerned that you're going to lose people's, you're going to lose momentum, but you're going to lose those capability and commitment to the program? Yeah, and that is, I mean, we have maintained engagement with the sector, and INZ has been maintaining engagement, but until I have a cabinet decision about the direction of travel, yeah, that is, but we are confident that the number of people that have been engaged in the trial program continue to be advocating for the program and I'm confident that they will continue to be available once we've made

cabinet decisions. Are you thinking you'll go to cabinet this year, in the next four years? Oh yes, definitely. And within this month I'm hoping to get through cabinet within this month and be able to make some announcements once. Thank you. Yeah, the question's been this. Yeah, something else, do you want to go?

Bye bye. Bye bye. I'll try to. Radio New Zealand reported recently that around 20 asylum seekers have been detained in corrections facilities. Since January 2024, how many asylum seekers have been physically accommodated in corrections facilities?

I think I'll have to refer to officials for the number. I can tell you that since the decision making, the freedom of movement decision making panel was established in 2022 that 21 individuals have claimed asylum while already subject to warrants of commitment with one individual claiming asylum during the process of obtaining a warrant.

So where a claimant is in detention under a warrant of commitment, when they claim asylum the panel's in principle decision in all cases is to have them released on conditions. However in some cases this kind of occur immediately because we have to find them suitable housing for which they can be released. So we try and do that as soon as possible.

I know that doesn't quite answer your question in terms of the timing, but. So since 2022, 21 individuals applied for asylum while they were already subject to a warrant of commitment. That's right. What kind of warrant of commitment and for example would they have been subject to?
I don't have that data, I'm afraid. But an immigration related warrant.

Okay, so the decision-making panel decided that asylum seekers should not be detained. At what length of time were these 21 individuals held? How long were they held in prison before? I think the specificity of that data would have to come back to you and WPQ.

We don't have it at hand. I don't have all the data for all 21. Did I hear correctly that the incentive of the policy would be to have people released as soon as possible? Yes, and I think there were some changes to the Immigration Act this February to improve protections for people that have claimed refugee or protected person status and are subject to a warrant of commitment.

So I think this was recommended in the KCW. So it has bespoke settings for the detention of asylum claimants and tries to take account of their particular vulnerabilities. So during this period, my right is thinking that there have been no detention of asylum seekers in corrections facilities unless they were already subject to a pre-existing warrant of commitment.

That's my understanding. OK. What are the circumstances where one of these 21 asylum seekers, what justification would there have been to keep them in prison? It's just a case of finding them accommodation that they can move to so we can't let them out to make them homeless.

So that's the consideration. We try and do it as fast as possible, but it depends a little bit about where in the country they are. If they're in Auckland, it's quite a lot easier. We have a... So do you think it's likely that it would be a few days or are we talking weeks or months that they would have been detained in corrections? I'd say a few days to two weeks, but not longer, typically.

It's certainly something we've had to do. And we can come back with the data for the next time. We'll ask that you, through the chair, that you would bring the committee a breakdown of, obviously, anonymised data of those 21 individuals. Certainly. How long, how many days they would tell them corrections for some of these, and what the justifications were.

Right? Yep. Right. Oh, it appears we have no further questions. Yeah, I do. You do? Oh, there we go. OK, Phil, you keep going. Yeah. So, Minister, in your opening remarks, you talked about what's been a pretty extraordinary deterioration internationally in the way that countries approach forced displacement.

The kind of bizarre outcome that New Zealand is now the third largest re-severment country, which never believed that five years ago or ten years ago. And I just, my question is really whether you or members of the department have what about what needs to change, given that the international environment's been deteriorating so sharply, the 1951 Convention is sort of more or less falling apart before our eyes.

And yet there's not quite a record. There's been a slight reduction in the number of people displaced globally, but it's still in the region of, you know, between 110 and 120 million people. How should a country like New Zealand be responding to this kind of crisis? It was actually, I had the privilege of visiting Geneva and visiting UNHCR and Red Cross earlier this year, I think it was.

And I think that there is, you know, in my discussions with UNHCR and you probably all have found it, there is huge work that can... be done to strengthen countries to allow people to remain or return. I think that that's a pathway of work that is important in this space. From my discussions with UNHCA and Red Cross, the economic component of those refugees, so it's moving because they can't grow food, etc., those sort of components is a significant issue. So I think the more we can do around our leaning into stabilising these environments so that people aren't forced to leave. I think there is

work in that space that will continue to be very important. I think the companion pathway work, which has now talked about cause, a number of countries are working on those companion pathways, those other options, so skill migration and those sort of beyond the refugee placement program, those other opportunities, workforce mobility, that sort of activity. So there is a range of things that I know and it is a reality. I don't think there's one fix, but I think that focusing on strengthening those vulnerable economies and those vulnerable places will

help to some extent. The other discussion in this has not been sort of policy work or anything, but one of the discussions that I thought was really useful was actually talking about those that are working in this space, particularly in the UNHCA and Red Cross environment, is that rather than everybody crossing over each other and everybody doing a little bit in the same place, is actually dividing up our responses. So different agencies in different countries focus on specific areas rather than us all doing a little bit in one place. There was some sort of

dialogue around that, which I think makes some sense around actually, you know, who's going to do what and what area, rather than us all going to one place and doing a lot and then moving to another place, you know, actually sharing that. And that came from the Red Cross initiatives in that space. So there is, you know, I think dialogue to have in that area.

Have you asked for any advice on complementary pathways, so labour market mobility or education as a way of... At this point, I'm really focused on getting the cause decisions through. And that is, you know, the first cab off the rank. I think that that is the priority for us to give stability in that space. Then moving forward. activity. So that becomes, you know, the next piece of work that I'm really focused on. It's up to me, Troy. It's just the new primary. Yeah, go for it. OK, your primary fail, Twyford. So there's been an ongoing debate over the last few years about how New Zealand can respond best to humanitarian emergencies and war. And most recently, I think, around Gaza, and your government has pretty steadfastly refused to consider a special humanitarian visa for the families of New Zealand.

those who were trapped in the war zone. Is there any process in place, or have you sought the advice, about how these crises should be treated in terms of a kind of rational and fair decision making around how New Zealand might respond to give refuge to families of New Zealanders who are brought up in those crises?

Well, the system that we have around UNHCR referrals, I think, has steadfastened an anchor of the refugee asylum process, of the refugee claim process. What I think we've seen is that bespoke systems or bespoke responses are problematic. They have created unintended consequences and issues when we have tried to do those.

I think the responses that we have had have been fair and reasonable. And that response that may sound good in theory, like we still have the same issues of how do we get people out, even if we've created it, all of those sort of processes. So no, I haven't sought specific advice.

I think that what we have done and how we respond within the framework we have has been effective. And that bespoke response is problematic. That doesn't constrain any future government to looking at different initiatives. How are they problematic?

In what way? So the Liffey talked about the Afghani refugee program that created, it was a long, slow process. It did effectively circumvented other people in the program, so those that were, because it was part of the 1500 quota. No, it was over and above.
